news_summary: Today's space news highlights exciting advancements in AI technology for autonomous spacecraft, groundbreaking discoveries about Mars' watery past, the potential to uncover millions of unseen neutron stars, and SpaceX's imminent launch of the tallest, most powerful rocket ever.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées passionnantes dans le domaine de l'intelligence artificielle appliquée aux engins spatiaux autonomes, des découvertes révolutionnaires sur le passé aquatique de Mars, la possibilité de découvrir des millions d'étoiles à neutrons encore inconnues, ainsi que le lancement imminent par SpaceX de la fusée la plus haute et la plus puissante jamais construite.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances emocionantes en la tecnología de inteligencia artificial para naves espaciales autónomas, descubrimientos revolucionarios sobre el pasado acuático de Marte, la posibilidad de descubrir millones de estrellas de neutrones hasta ahora desconocidas y el inminente lanzamiento por parte de SpaceX del cohete más alto y potente de la historia.

articles.title: NASA’s new AI space chip could let spacecraft think for themselves
articles.summary: NASA is testing a next-generation space computer chip that could give spacecraft the ability to operate far more independently in deep space. The radiation-hardened processor is showing performance levels hundreds of times beyond current spaceflight computers while surviving punishing tests designed to mimic the harsh conditions of space. The technology could enable AI-powered spacecraft, faster scientific discoveries, and smarter missions to the Moon and Mars.
articles.content:
NASA is developing a powerful new computer chip designed to dramatically increase the intelligence and performance of future spacecraft. Through a commercial partnership, the project is creating advanced processing technology capable of helping spacecraft operate more independently during missions far from Earth.

NASA's High Performance Spaceflight Computing project is focused on boosting the computing capabilities of spacecraft used in space exploration. Current missions rely on older processors because they are durable enough to survive the extreme conditions of space. While those chips are dependable, they lack the performance needed for more advanced missions.

The agency says newer and far more capable processors are essential for future autonomous spacecraft, faster onboard scientific analysis, and supporting astronauts during missions to the Moon and Mars.

"Building on the legacy of previous space processors, this new multicore system is fault-tolerant, flexible, and extremely high-performing," said Eugene Schwanbeck, program element manager in NASA's Game Changing Development program at the agency's Langley Research Center, in Hampton, Virginia. "NASA's commitment to advancing spaceflight computing is a triumph of technical achievement and collaboration."

Radiation Hardened Processor Faces Extreme Testing

At the center of the project is a new radiation-hardened processor built to deliver up to 100 times the computing power of today's spaceflight computers while surviving the harsh environment of space. Engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California are running a wide range of tests designed to simulate those conditions.

"We are putting these new chips through the wringer by carrying out radiation, thermal, and shock tests while also evaluating their performance through a rigorous functional test campaign," said Jim Butler, High Performance Space Computing project manager at JPL.

To qualify for spaceflight, the processor must withstand intense electromagnetic radiation and dramatic temperature fluctuations that can damage electronics. High-energy particles from the Sun and deep space can also trigger computer errors that force spacecraft into "safe mode," temporarily shutting down nonessential systems until engineers resolve the issue.

NASA is also testing how the chip handles the challenges of planetary landings.

"To simulate real-world performance, we are using high-fidelity landing scenarios from real NASA missions that would typically require power-intensive hardware to process huge volumes of landing-sensor data," said Butler. "This is an exciting time for us to be working on hardware that will enable NASA's next giant leaps."

Testing at JPL began in February and is expected to continue for several months. Early results have been highly encouraging. According to NASA, the processor is functioning as intended and has shown performance levels roughly 500 times greater than the radiation-hardened chips currently used in spacecraft.

The team also marked the beginning of testing with a symbolic moment by sending an email titled "Hello Universe," referencing the famous introductory messages used during the early days of computer programming.

AI Powered Spacecraft and Deep Space Missions

The processor is being developed jointly by JPL and Microchip Technology Inc., based in Chandler, Arizona. The company is working with NASA through a commercial partnership, and sample chips have already been shared with defense and commercial aerospace partners.

The technology is expected to play a major role in the future of autonomous spacecraft. With onboard artificial intelligence, spacecraft could respond to unexpected situations in real time when communication delays make human control impractical. The chip could also help deep space missions process, store, and transmit massive amounts of scientific data back to Earth more efficiently.

NASA says the processor may eventually support crewed missions to the Moon and Mars as well.

Small Processor With Massive Computing Power

The device is known as a system-on-a-chip (or SoC), meaning it combines the essential components of a computer into a single compact unit. The processor includes central processing units, computational offloads, advanced networking systems, memory, and input/output interfaces.

SoCs are widely used in smartphones and tablets because they are compact and energy efficient. However, NASA's version is designed to survive for years in deep space, potentially traveling millions (or even billions) of miles from Earth without maintenance or repairs.

Once the processor is certified for use in space, NASA plans to integrate it into a wide variety of missions, including Earth orbiters, planetary rovers, deep space probes, and crewed habitats.

The technology could also have benefits on Earth. Microchip plans to adapt the processor for industries such as aviation and automotive manufacturing.

NASA and Industry Collaboration

The project is managed by the Space Technology Mission Directorate's Game Changing Development (GCD) program at NASA Langley. The GCD program and JPL, which is managed by Caltech in Pasadena, California, oversaw the development process from mission planning and industry studies through final delivery.

NASA JPL selected Microchip as a partner in 2022, and the company funded its own research and development work on the processor.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough in spaceflight computing with a new radiation-hardened AI chip that dramatically improves spacecraft autonomy and performance, enabling smarter deep space missions and supporting future crewed missions to the Moon and Mars. The development has broad implications for space exploration and potential terrestrial applications, with detailed context and testing results provided.
articles.personality_title: NASA develops powerful AI chip to help spacecraft operate independently
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on better computer chips for spacecraft. Current space computers are strong but slow, limiting what missions can do far from Earth. New chips are needed to help spacecraft think and act on their own during long trips to places like the Moon and Mars.

**What happened** – NASA created a new radiation-hardened chip that can handle space’s harsh conditions like radiation and temperature changes. This chip is about 500 times faster than the ones used now. Engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory have been testing it since February, checking if it can survive shocks and intense radiation while working properly.

**Impact** – This chip could let spacecraft make decisions without waiting for instructions from Earth, which is important because signals take a long time to travel in space. It will help spacecraft analyze scientific data faster and improve missions to explore planets and moons. The chip is small but very powerful and can last for years in deep space.

**What's next step** – After testing finishes, NASA plans to use this chip in many future missions, including satellites around Earth, rovers on other planets, and crewed missions to the Moon and Mars. The company making the chip also wants to adapt it for airplanes and cars on Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new AI-powered space chip could make spacecraft smarter and more independent, enabling better exploration of deep space and support for future astronauts.
articles.personality_title_fr: La puce IA puissante de la NASA pour rendre les engins spatiaux plus autonomes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille sur de meilleures puces informatiques pour les engins spatiaux. Les ordinateurs spatiaux actuels sont solides mais lents, ce qui limite les missions lointaines. De nouvelles puces sont nécessaires pour que les engins puissent penser et agir seuls lors de longs voyages vers la Lune ou Mars.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La NASA a créé une nouvelle puce résistante aux radiations capable de supporter les conditions difficiles de l’espace, comme la radiation et les changements de température. Cette puce est environ 500 fois plus rapide que celles utilisées actuellement. Les ingénieurs du Jet Propulsion Laboratory de la NASA la testent depuis février pour vérifier sa résistance aux chocs et aux radiations tout en fonctionnant correctement.

**Impact** – Cette puce pourrait permettre aux engins spatiaux de prendre des décisions sans attendre les instructions de la Terre, ce qui est important car les signaux mettent beaucoup de temps à voyager dans l’espace. Elle aidera les engins à analyser plus vite les données scientifiques et à améliorer les missions d’exploration des planètes et des lunes. La puce est petite mais très puissante et peut durer des années dans l’espace profond.

**Étape suivante** – Après les tests, la NASA prévoit d’utiliser cette puce dans de nombreuses missions futures, comme les satellites autour de la Terre, les rovers sur d’autres planètes, et les missions habitées vers la Lune et Mars. La société qui fabrique la puce souhaite aussi l’adapter pour les avions et les voitures sur Terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle puce spatiale intelligente de la NASA pourrait rendre les engins spatiaux plus autonomes et efficaces, améliorant l’exploration de l’espace lointain et le soutien aux astronautes.
articles.personality_title_es: La nueva chip IA de la NASA ayuda a que las naves espaciales sean más autónomas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está desarrollando mejores chips para las naves espaciales. Las computadoras actuales son fuertes pero lentas, limitando las misiones lejos de la Tierra. Se necesitan nuevos chips para que las naves puedan pensar y actuar por sí mismas en largos viajes a la Luna y Marte.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA creó un nuevo chip resistente a radiación que puede soportar las duras condiciones del espacio, como radiación y cambios de temperatura. Este chip es unas 500 veces más rápido que los que se usan ahora. Los ingenieros del Laboratorio de Propulsión a Chorro de la NASA lo están probando desde febrero para asegurarse de que aguante golpes y radiación mientras funciona bien.

**Impacto** – Este chip podría permitir que las naves espaciales tomen decisiones sin esperar instrucciones de la Tierra, lo cual es importante porque las señales tardan mucho en viajar en el espacio. Ayudará a analizar datos científicos más rápido y mejorará las misiones para explorar planetas y lunas. El chip es pequeño pero muy potente y puede durar años en el espacio profundo.

**Próximo paso** – Después de las pruebas, la NASA planea usar este chip en muchas misiones futuras, como satélites alrededor de la Tierra, rovers en otros planetas, y misiones tripuladas a la Luna y Marte. La empresa que fabrica el chip también quiere adaptarlo para aviones y autos en la Tierra.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo chip espacial con IA de la NASA podría hacer que las naves sean más inteligentes y autónomas, permitiendo una mejor exploración del espacio profundo y apoyo a futuros astronautas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sleek, glowing computer chip shaped like a futuristic spacecraft core, radiating intricate circuits that morph into softly illuminated stars and planets, set against a calm cosmic background with gentle hues of deep blue, soft orange, and muted silver, symbolizing advanced AI-powered space exploration and autonomous deep-space missions.

articles.title: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could reveal millions of invisible neutron stars
articles.summary: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could expose a vast hidden population of neutron stars lurking unseen across the Milky Way. By detecting subtle shifts in starlight caused by gravity, the mission may identify and even weigh isolated neutron stars that are otherwise impossible to see. Scientists hope the discoveries will reveal how these extreme objects are born and why they are blasted through space at incredible speeds.
articles.content:
Astronomers have long believed the Milky Way is filled with neutron stars, the ultra dense remnants left behind when massive stars explode. The problem is that most of these objects are nearly impossible to see. A new study published in Astronomy and Astrophysics suggests NASA's upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope may finally be able to uncover some of them.

Using advanced simulations of the Milky Way and predictions of Roman's future observations, researchers found the space telescope could detect and study dozens of isolated neutron stars through a phenomenon known as gravitational microlensing.

"Most neutron stars are relatively dim and on their own," said Zofia Kaczmarek of Heidelberg University in Germany, who led the study. "They are incredibly hard to spot without some sort of help."

How Roman Could Detect Invisible Neutron Stars

Neutron stars contain more mass than the Sun packed into an object roughly the size of a city. Scientists study them to better understand how stars evolve, explode, and distribute heavy elements throughout the cosmos. They also offer a rare opportunity to investigate matter under the most extreme conditions (pressures and densities) imaginable.

Most neutron stars remain hidden unless they appear as pulsars that emit radio waves or shine brightly in X-rays. Even the most powerful telescopes can miss isolated neutron stars that produce little or no detectable light.

The Roman Space Telescope could find them indirectly. When a massive object such as a neutron star passes in front of a more distant star, its gravity bends and magnifies the background star's light. This effect, called microlensing, temporarily makes the distant star appear brighter and slightly shifted in the sky.

Many telescopes can detect the brief brightening caused by microlensing, but Roman is expected to do much more. The observatory will precisely measure both the increase in brightness (photometry) and the tiny positional movement (astrometry) of the background star.

Because neutron stars are relatively heavy, they create a stronger astrometric signal than smaller objects. That means Roman may not only detect hidden neutron stars, but also measure their masses, something extremely difficult to achieve using photometry alone.

"What's really cool about using microlensing is that you can get direct mass measurements," said paper co-author Peter McGill of Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. "Photometry tells us that something passed in front of the star, but it's the amount the star's position shifts that tells us how massive that object is. By measuring that tiny deflection on the sky, we can directly weigh something that is otherwise unseen."

Solving Neutron Star Mysteries

Roman's observations could help scientists answer major questions about neutron stars and black holes, including whether there is a true gap between their masses. The mission may also reveal how quickly neutron stars travel through the galaxy.

Researchers are especially interested in the powerful "kicks" neutron stars receive during supernova explosions. These violent events can launch them through space at hundreds of miles per second.

The team plans to use Roman's future Galactic Bulge Time Domain Survey, which will repeatedly observe millions of stars in enormous sections of the sky.

"We're going to get to work as soon as the data start coming in," said McGill. "Even in the first months after commissioning, we expect to start identifying promising events."

Even a modest number of confirmed discoveries could significantly improve models of stellar explosions and the behavior of matter under extreme conditions.

"We don't know the mass distribution of neutron stars, black holes, or where one ends and the other begins with any certainty," McGill said. "Roman will really be a breakthrough in that."

A Hidden Population Waiting To Be Found

So far, astronomers have identified only a few thousand neutron stars, most of them detected as pulsars. However, scientists estimate the Milky Way could contain anywhere from tens of millions to hundreds of millions of neutron stars. Researchers have also only been able to measure neutron star masses in binary systems where two objects orbit each other.

"We're seeing a small sample that's not representative of the big picture," Kaczmarek said. "Even a single mass measurement would be very powerful. If we found just one isolated neutron star, it would already be incredibly stimulating to our research."

The study also highlights an unexpected scientific advantage of the Roman mission. Although the telescope's survey was originally designed mainly to discover exoplanets through photometric microlensing, its advanced astrometric precision may open the door to entirely new kinds of discoveries.

"This wasn't part of the original plan," said McGill. "But it turns out Roman's astrometric capability is really good at detecting neutron stars and black holes, so we can add a whole new kind of science to Roman's surveys."

If the predictions are correct, Roman could deliver the first large collection of isolated neutron stars detected purely through their gravitational effects. The mission is expected to dramatically expand the study of microlensing and uncover hidden populations of objects throughout the Milky Way, including rogue planets and stellar remnants such as neutron stars.

The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope is managed at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, with participation by NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California; Caltech/IPAC in Pasadena, California; the Space Telescope Science Institute in Baltimore; and a science team comprising scientists from various research institutions. The primary industrial partners are BAE Systems Inc. in Boulder, Colorado; L3Harris Technologies in Rochester, New York; and Teledyne Scientific & Imaging in Thousand Oaks, California.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant upcoming NASA space mission, the Roman Space Telescope, which could reveal millions of previously invisible neutron stars through gravitational microlensing. This discovery would have broad scientific impact by advancing understanding of stellar evolution, neutron star properties, and extreme matter conditions. The story focuses on a single, well-explained scientific breakthrough with large-scale implications for astrophysics and space science.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope may uncover millions of hidden neutron stars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Neutron stars are extremely dense remains of massive stars that exploded long ago. Although many exist in our Milky Way galaxy, most are invisible because they give off little or no light, making them very hard to find.

**What happened** – NASA’s upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will use a special technique called gravitational microlensing to detect these hidden neutron stars. When a neutron star passes in front of a distant star, its gravity bends and brightens the star’s light. Roman will measure both this brightening and tiny shifts in the star’s position, allowing scientists to find and weigh neutron stars that no other telescope can see.

**Impact** – This new method could reveal dozens of isolated neutron stars and help scientists learn how they are formed and why many move at very high speeds through space. It also offers a way to measure neutron star masses directly, which has been nearly impossible for stars not in pairs. This will improve our understanding of how stars explode and how matter behaves in extreme conditions.

**What's next step** – The Roman Space Telescope will begin a survey called the Galactic Bulge Time Domain Survey, observing millions of stars repeatedly. Scientists expect to start finding neutron stars soon after the telescope begins its work, opening a new chapter in studying these mysterious objects.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Roman Space Telescope will use gravity’s effects on starlight to discover and weigh many hidden neutron stars, deepening our knowledge of the universe’s most extreme objects.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope spatial Roman de la NASA pourrait révéler des millions d’étoiles à neutrons invisibles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les étoiles à neutrons sont des restes extrêmement denses d’étoiles massives qui ont explosé il y a longtemps. Bien que beaucoup existent dans notre galaxie, la Voie lactée, la plupart sont invisibles car elles émettent peu ou pas de lumière, ce qui les rend très difficiles à détecter.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le futur télescope spatial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA utilisera une technique spéciale appelée microlentille gravitationnelle pour détecter ces étoiles à neutrons cachées. Lorsqu’une étoile à neutrons passe devant une étoile lointaine, sa gravité courbe et éclaire la lumière de cette étoile. Le télescope Roman mesurera à la fois cet éclaircissement et de minuscules déplacements dans la position de l’étoile, permettant aux scientifiques de trouver et de peser des étoiles à neutrons que aucun autre télescope ne peut voir.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode pourrait révéler des dizaines d’étoiles à neutrons isolées et aider les scientifiques à comprendre comment elles se forment et pourquoi beaucoup se déplacent à très grande vitesse dans l’espace. Elle offre aussi un moyen de mesurer directement la masse des étoiles à neutrons, ce qui était presque impossible pour celles qui ne sont pas en binaires. Cela améliorera notre compréhension des explosions stellaires et du comportement de la matière dans des conditions extrêmes.

**Prochaine étape** – Le télescope Roman commencera une enquête appelée le relevé temporel du bulbe galactique, en observant des millions d’étoiles à plusieurs reprises. Les scientifiques s’attendent à commencer à trouver des étoiles à neutrons dès le début de la mission, ouvrant un nouveau chapitre dans l’étude de ces objets mystérieux.

**Une phrase clé** – Le télescope spatial Roman de la NASA utilisera les effets de la gravité sur la lumière des étoiles pour découvrir et peser de nombreuses étoiles à neutrons cachées, approfondissant ainsi notre connaissance des objets les plus extrêmes de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio espacial Roman de la NASA podría descubrir millones de estrellas de neutrones invisibles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las estrellas de neutrones son restos extremadamente densos de estrellas masivas que explotaron hace mucho tiempo. Aunque muchas existen en nuestra galaxia, la Vía Láctea, la mayoría son invisibles porque emiten poca o ninguna luz, por lo que son muy difíciles de encontrar.

**Qué pasó** – El próximo telescopio espacial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA utilizará una técnica especial llamada microlente gravitacional para detectar estas estrellas de neutrones ocultas. Cuando una estrella de neutrones pasa frente a una estrella más lejana, su gravedad dobla y aumenta la luz de esa estrella. Roman medirá tanto este brillo como pequeños movimientos en la posición de la estrella, permitiendo a los científicos encontrar y pesar estrellas de neutrones que ningún otro telescopio puede ver.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método podría revelar docenas de estrellas de neutrones aisladas y ayudar a los científicos a entender cómo se forman y por qué muchas se mueven a altas velocidades por el espacio. También ofrece una manera de medir directamente la masa de las estrellas de neutrones, algo casi imposible para las que no están en sistemas binarios. Esto mejorará nuestro conocimiento sobre cómo explotan las estrellas y cómo se comporta la materia en condiciones extremas.

**Próximo paso** – El telescopio Roman comenzará una encuesta llamada Estudio del Tiempo del Bulbo Galáctico, observando millones de estrellas repetidamente. Los científicos esperan comenzar a encontrar estrellas de neutrones poco después de que el telescopio inicie su trabajo, abriendo un nuevo capítulo en el estudio de estos objetos misteriosos.

**Una frase clave** – El telescopio espacial Roman de la NASA usará los efectos de la gravedad sobre la luz de las estrellas para descubrir y pesar muchas estrellas de neutrones ocultas, profundizando nuestro conocimiento sobre los objetos más extremos del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast starry Milky Way galaxy with glowing, dense neutron star remnants depicted as small, luminous city-sized orbs subtly bending and magnifying the light of distant stars behind them, illustrating gravitational microlensing with gentle arcs of light distortion and soft positional shifts in star patterns, all rendered in natural, muted colors.

articles.title: SpaceX is about to launch tallest and most powerful rocket in history
articles.summary: A record-breaking new version of Starship, due to launch within days, could form the basis of NASA's ambitious Artemis programme that aims to put humans back on the moon as soon as 2028
articles.content:
A record-breaking new version of Starship, due to launch within days, could form the basis of NASA's ambitious Artemis programme that aims to put humans back on the moon as soon as 2028

Starship on the launchpad at SpaceX’s Starbase in Texas SpaceX

SpaceX will fly an extensively upgraded Starship next week that will – if it launches successfully – break records as the tallest and most powerful rocket in history. The flight will be watched keenly at NASA, as the craft is vital to its plans to put humans back on the moon in 2028.

Starship is made up of two parts: an upper stage, confusingly also called Starship, and a lower stage called Super Heavy. Since the last Starship test in October last year, SpaceX has been making extensive revisions to both stages.

Advertisement

The twelfth test flight, which is expected to launch as soon as 19 May, will involve new version 3 models of both craft. Powering each stage will be version 3 Raptor engines, which have seen limited testing on previous flights, and the launch will take place from a newly designed pad at the company’s Starbase site in Texas, meaning that the stakes for the test are particularly high.

On Super Heavy 3, the number of grid fins, intended to steer it back through the atmosphere to a safe landing, has been reduced from four to three but their size is expanded by 50 per cent. Starship 3 has a new, larger propellant tank, equipment for in-orbit refuelling and improved heat-resistant tiles for atmospheric re-entry.

The total height of the rocket at launch will be 124 metres – about 1 metre taller than version 2 of Starship. Its height also surpasses the 98-metre-tall Space Launch System (SLS) rocket currently used by NASA and the 111-metre-tall Saturn V that sent astronauts to the moon in the 1960s and 70s.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

Starship 3 will also have 75,000 kilonewtons of thrust, which is almost twice the 39,000 kilonewtons of SLS, making it the most powerful rocket ever launched.

Alistair John at the University of Sheffield, UK, has calculated that the power of all the engines on the full Starship stack, at peak output, is larger than that produced by all electricity generation in Germany. “It’s massive,” says John.

Starship is being developed to put satellites in orbit and also ultimately to run missions to Mars, according to SpaceX CEO Elon Musk. But it has also been selected by NASA as one of two commercial lander designs for its Artemis programme to return humans to the moon, alongside a lander from Jeff Bezos-backed Blue Origin.

After an uncrewed Artemis I mission in 2022, the second Artemis flight earlier this year took four astronauts around the moon, further from Earth than any human has travelled before.

A recently released NASA document confirms that the Artemis III mission will see crew launch in an Orion spacecraft atop the SLS rocket into low Earth orbit and then rendezvous with “one or both commercial lunar landers provided by SpaceX and Blue Origin”. Such a manoeuvre will be needed to get crew and fuel aboard a lander ahead of a mission to land on the moon’s surface, with Artemis IV aiming to do this as early as 2028.

SpaceX is using a fail-fast, learn-fast strategy more common in Silicon Valley than the conservative world of space exploration. Out of the 11 previous test flights, there have been six successful flights and five failures. Neither SpaceX nor NASA responded to a request for comment.

Peter Shaw at Kingston University London believes SpaceX is on track for the Artemis programme, despite previous failures. “Rocket science is difficult. It’s challenging. It’s complex,” says Shaw. “Can they do it? Yes. Can they do it within the timeline? There’s a lot to be quietly confident about. Even if you have another failure or two, or five… they’ll learn from it, they’ll iterate it and they’ll put a new system together.”

John says the upcoming Starship test will be important to verify the design of the version 3 craft that will form the basis of the the Human Lander System (HLS) that SpaceX intends to touch down on the moon. HLS will need significant alterations, such as different engines designed to touch down under the moon’s lower gravity, and no heat shield, as it will never have to withstand re-entry to Earth’s atmosphere.

“It’s in a way small, incremental improvements, but then also it’s by far the most significant version: this version 3 is what they need for the Artemis programme. The other ones have been prototypes,” says John. “Version 3 is really the first test of the production model. Now it’s just making it reliable.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the imminent launch of the tallest and most powerful rocket in history, SpaceX's upgraded Starship version 3, which is critical to NASA's Artemis programme aiming to return humans to the moon by 2028. This represents a significant technological breakthrough with broad implications for space exploration and human spaceflight, providing measurable benefits for society's scientific and exploratory ambitions. The article provides detailed context about the rocket's capabilities, its role in NASA's plans, and the potential impact on future missions.
articles.personality_title: SpaceX prepares to launch tallest, most powerful rocket for NASA moon mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – SpaceX is developing a giant rocket called Starship, which is designed to carry people and cargo into space. NASA plans to use Starship as part of its Artemis programme to send astronauts back to the moon by 2028.

**What happened** – SpaceX is about to launch an upgraded version of Starship, called version 3, from its Texas site. This rocket will be 124 meters tall, making it the tallest and most powerful rocket ever built. It has new engines and improved features to help it fly and land safely. The launch is expected soon, and it will test the design important for NASA’s moon mission.

**Impact** – This launch is important because Starship will be stronger and bigger than any rocket before it, including NASA’s current rockets. It will carry more fuel and equipment, which is needed for long space trips. NASA will use Starship to land astronauts on the moon, making it a key step toward returning humans there after many years.

**What's next step** – If this test flight goes well, SpaceX will continue improving Starship to make it reliable for crewed missions. NASA plans to use it alongside other spacecraft to send astronauts to the moon’s surface by 2028. Future flights will focus on adapting Starship for moon landings and possibly even Mars missions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – SpaceX’s upcoming launch of the upgraded Starship rocket marks a major step toward NASA’s goal of returning humans to the moon within the next few years.
articles.personality_title_fr: SpaceX prépare le lancement de la plus haute et puissante fusée pour la mission lunaire de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – SpaceX développe une énorme fusée appelée Starship, conçue pour transporter des personnes et du matériel dans l’espace. La NASA prévoit d’utiliser Starship dans le cadre de son programme Artemis pour renvoyer des astronautes sur la Lune d’ici 2028.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – SpaceX s’apprête à lancer une version améliorée de Starship, appelée version 3, depuis son site au Texas. Cette fusée mesurera 124 mètres de haut, ce qui en fera la plus haute et la plus puissante jamais construite. Elle dispose de nouveaux moteurs et de caractéristiques améliorées pour voler et atterrir en toute sécurité. Le lancement est prévu prochainement et testera un design important pour la mission lunaire de la NASA.

**Impact** – Ce lancement est important car Starship sera plus grande et plus puissante que toutes les fusées précédentes, y compris celles actuelles de la NASA. Elle pourra transporter plus de carburant et d’équipement, ce qui est nécessaire pour les longs voyages spatiaux. La NASA utilisera Starship pour poser des astronautes sur la Lune, ce qui représente une étape clé pour y retourner après de nombreuses années.

**Prochaine étape** – Si ce vol d’essai réussit, SpaceX continuera à améliorer Starship pour le rendre fiable pour les missions avec équipage. La NASA prévoit de l’utiliser avec d’autres engins pour envoyer des astronautes sur la surface lunaire d’ici 2028. Les vols futurs viseront à adapter Starship pour les atterrissages lunaires et peut-être même pour des missions vers Mars.

**Phrase clé** – Le lancement imminent du Starship amélioré par SpaceX marque une étape majeure vers l’objectif de la NASA de ramener des humains sur la Lune dans les prochaines années.
articles.personality_title_es: SpaceX se prepara para lanzar el cohete más alto y potente para la misión lunar de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – SpaceX está desarrollando un cohete gigante llamado Starship, diseñado para llevar personas y carga al espacio. La NASA planea usar Starship como parte de su programa Artemis para enviar astronautas de regreso a la luna para 2028.

**Qué pasó** – SpaceX está a punto de lanzar una versión mejorada de Starship, llamada versión 3, desde su base en Texas. Este cohete tendrá 124 metros de altura, siendo el más alto y potente jamás construido. Cuenta con nuevos motores y mejoras para volar y aterrizar con seguridad. El lanzamiento está previsto pronto y probará un diseño clave para la misión lunar de la NASA.

**Impacto** – Este lanzamiento es importante porque Starship será más grande y fuerte que cualquier cohete anterior, incluyendo los actuales de la NASA. Podrá transportar más combustible y equipo, lo que es necesario para viajes espaciales largos. La NASA usará Starship para aterrizar astronautas en la luna, lo que es un paso esencial para regresar después de muchos años.

**Próximo paso** – Si este vuelo de prueba tiene éxito, SpaceX seguirá mejorando Starship para hacerlo confiable en misiones con tripulación. La NASA planea usarlo junto con otras naves para enviar astronautas a la superficie lunar para 2028. Los vuelos futuros se centrarán en adaptar Starship para aterrizajes en la luna y posiblemente para misiones a Marte.

**Frase clave** – El próximo lanzamiento del Starship mejorado de SpaceX es un paso importante hacia la meta de la NASA de regresar humanos a la luna en los próximos años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a towering, sleek rocket with two distinct stacked sections—a larger lower booster stage with three oversized grid fins and a slimmer upper stage featuring a gleaming, heat-resistant surface—standing proudly on a minimalist launchpad set against a soft Texas dawn sky, symbolizing breakthrough space exploration and human ambition to reach the moon.

articles.title: Mars may have once had an ocean and this chaotic valley is a big clue
articles.summary: A colossal valley near Mars’s equator is revealing dramatic clues about the Red Planet’s watery and volcanic past. Stretching roughly 1,300 kilometers, Shalbatana Vallis was carved billions of years ago when enormous floods of groundwater burst onto the surface, gouging deep winding channels across the landscape. Today, the region is a striking mix of ancient flood scars, collapsed “chaotic terrain,” lava-smoothed plains, volcanic ash, and battered impact craters — all hinting at a Mars that may once have been far warmer and wetter than it is now.
articles.content:
ESA's Mars Express mission is spotlighting Shalbatana Vallis this month, an enormous Martian valley marked by evidence of ancient water, volcanic activity, impact craters, and collapsed terrain.

Located near the equator of Mars, Shalbatana Vallis stretches across the planet for roughly 1,300 kilometers, about the length of Italy. The latest image from the spacecraft's High Resolution Stereo Camera (HRSC) focuses on the northern section of the valley as it winds across the Martian landscape.

In October 2025, Mars Express also released a video tour of this remarkable region, tracing the valley's path from its source in the highlands of Xanthe Terra to its endpoint in the smoother terrain of Chryse Planitia.

Ancient Floods Shaped Shalbatana Vallis

Scientists believe Shalbatana Vallis formed around 3.5 billion years ago after vast amounts of groundwater burst onto the surface. The resulting floods tore through the landscape, carving deep channels as the water rushed downhill.

The main valley visible in the image enters from the lower left and exits toward the north on the right side of the frame. It measures roughly 10 kilometers wide and reaches depths of around 500 meters, features that are especially clear in the accompanying topographic view.

Researchers think the valley was once even deeper than it appears today. Over billions of years, various materials gradually filled parts of the channel. One especially noticeable blue-black patch in the rougher section of the valley is believed to be volcanic ash that was later redistributed by Martian winds, as seen in the accompanying 3D perspective views.

Evidence of Water, Lava, and a Possible Ancient Ocean

Shalbatana Vallis is one of many outflow channels found in this part of Mars. The region marks the transition between the heavily cratered southern highlands (to the left) and the smoother northern lowlands (right).

Nearby lies Chryse Planitia, one of the lowest regions on Mars (see map). Many of the planet's major outflow channels end there, leading some scientists to suggest that the area may once have contained a large ocean during a warmer and wetter period in Mars's history.

Chaotic Terrain and Impact Craters

The area surrounding Shalbatana Vallis contains many additional geological features, all labeled in the annotated image of the region.

Outflow channels on Mars are often associated with so-called chaotic terrain, landscapes filled with broken blocks, ridges, and irregular mounds of rock. In this image, chaotic terrain appears in the broader section of the valley near the dark volcanic ash deposits (see image at the top of this article).

Scientists think this type of terrain formed when underground ice began melting. As the ice disappeared, the ground above it shifted and collapsed. Similar chaotic landscapes have been observed by Mars Express in areas including Pyrrhae Regio, Iani Chaos, Ariadnes Colles, Aram Chaos, and Hydraotes Chaos.

Numerous impact craters are also visible throughout the region. Some remain sharply defined, while others have been partially buried or eroded away over time. Several are surrounded by ejecta blankets, debris blasted outward during the original impacts.

The smoother appearance of much of the terrain suggests that lava once flowed across the region. As the lava cooled and contracted, it folded and crumpled, producing uneven features known as 'wrinkle ridges'. Isolated hills called 'mesas' are also visible (to the upper right, for example). These are remnants of older elevated surfaces that have slowly eroded over time.

More Than Two Decades of Mars Exploration

The image was captured by the HRSC camera, one of eight scientific instruments aboard Mars Express. Since launching in 2003, the spacecraft has spent more than 20 years studying the Red Planet and mapping its surface in color and in three dimensions at unprecedented detail.

Data collected by Mars Express has significantly expanded scientists' understanding of Mars and its geological history.

The Mars Express HRSC was developed and is operated by the German Aerospace Center (Deutsches Zentrum für Luft- und Raumfahrt; DLR). Camera data processing was carried out at the DLR Institute of Space Research in Berlin-Adlershof. Researchers in the Planetary Science and Remote Sensing group at Freie Universität Berlin used the data to create the image products shown here.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about Mars' ancient watery and volcanic past, providing meaningful insights into planetary history that inspire curiosity and hope for future exploration. The findings have broad significance for understanding Mars and potential past habitability, supported by detailed data from a long-term space mission.
articles.personality_title: New images reveal ancient floods and volcanic activity on Mars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mars Express, a spacecraft orbiting Mars since 2003, has been studying the planet’s surface to learn about its history. Scientists are especially interested in places where water once flowed and volcanic activity happened.

**What happened** – Recently, Mars Express took detailed pictures of Shalbatana Vallis, a huge valley near Mars’s equator that stretches about 1,300 kilometers. This valley was formed around 3.5 billion years ago when massive floods of groundwater burst onto the surface, carving deep channels. The images show features like volcanic ash, collapsed terrain, lava-smoothed plains, and impact craters.

**Impact** – These findings suggest Mars was once warmer and wetter, with flowing water and volcanic activity shaping its surface. The valley’s connection to a lowland area called Chryse Planitia hints that a large ancient ocean might have existed there. Understanding these features helps scientists learn how Mars changed over time and if it could have supported life.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying Mars Express data and use it alongside other missions to explore more valleys and plains. This research may guide future missions that aim to find signs of past life or prepare for human exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New images of Shalbatana Vallis give strong evidence that ancient floods and volcanoes shaped Mars, revealing clues about its watery past and possible ancient ocean.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles images révèlent d’anciennes inondations et activités volcaniques sur Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Mars Express, une sonde en orbite autour de Mars depuis 2003, étudie la surface de la planète pour comprendre son histoire. Les scientifiques s’intéressent particulièrement aux endroits où l’eau a coulé et où il y a eu des volcans.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, Mars Express a pris des images détaillées de Shalbatana Vallis, une énorme vallée près de l’équateur martien qui s’étend sur environ 1 300 kilomètres. Cette vallée s’est formée il y a environ 3,5 milliards d’années lorsque d’immenses inondations d’eau souterraine ont déferlé à la surface, creusant des canaux profonds. Les images montrent des cendres volcaniques, un terrain effondré, des plaines lissées par la lave et des cratères d’impact.

**Impact** – Ces découvertes indiquent que Mars était autrefois plus chaud et plus humide, avec de l’eau qui coulait et des volcans qui modifiaient sa surface. La vallée, reliée à une zone basse appelée Chryse Planitia, suggère qu’un grand océan ancien pourrait y avoir existé. Comprendre ces caractéristiques aide les scientifiques à savoir comment Mars a changé avec le temps et s’il a pu abriter la vie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront d’étudier les données de Mars Express et les combineront avec d’autres missions pour explorer d’autres vallées et plaines. Ces recherches pourraient orienter de futures missions cherchant des signes de vie passée ou préparant l’exploration humaine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles images de Shalbatana Vallis apportent des preuves solides que d’anciennes inondations et volcans ont façonné Mars, révélant des indices sur son passé aqueux et un océan ancien possible.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas imágenes revelan antiguas inundaciones y actividad volcánica en Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Mars Express, una nave espacial que orbita Marte desde 2003, ha estado estudiando la superficie del planeta para entender su historia. Los científicos están interesados en lugares donde hubo agua y actividad volcánica.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, Mars Express tomó imágenes detalladas de Shalbatana Vallis, un enorme valle cerca del ecuador marciano que mide aproximadamente 1.300 kilómetros. Este valle se formó hace unos 3.500 millones de años cuando grandes inundaciones de agua subterránea brotaron a la superficie, creando canales profundos. Las imágenes muestran ceniza volcánica, terreno colapsado, llanuras suavizadas por lava y cráteres de impacto.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos indican que Marte fue alguna vez más cálido y húmedo, con agua corriente y actividad volcánica que moldearon su superficie. La conexión del valle con una zona baja llamada Chryse Planitia sugiere que pudo haber existido un gran océano antiguo. Entender estas características ayuda a los científicos a aprender cómo cambió Marte y si pudo haber tenido vida.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando los datos de Mars Express y los combinarán con otras misiones para explorar más valles y llanuras. Esta investigación podría guiar futuras misiones que busquen señales de vida pasada o preparen la exploración humana.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevas imágenes de Shalbatana Vallis ofrecen evidencias claras de que antiguas inundaciones y volcanes moldearon Marte, mostrando pistas sobre su pasado con agua y un posible océano antiguo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast Martian valley with winding deep channels carved by ancient floods, showing layered rocky cliffs and scattered dark volcanic ash patches, transitioning from rugged chaotic terrain with collapsed blocks to smoother plains dotted with mesas and crater outlines under a soft reddish-orange sky.
